sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 17, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see: after SPACE, id=13 is white (100px) again. So SPACE merged id=13 (purple) and id=14 (white) back into one white object. The merge took the color from the BOTTOM object (id=14 was white).
Current state: id=12 (white, 84px), id=13 (white, 100px)
Left panel: white above purple
So the center is now white-white, but the left panel is white-purple. I need the bottom to be purple.
The pattern seems to be:
1. SPACE splits the bottom object into two halves
2. I can paint the top object (id=12) via indicators
3. But how do I paint the bottom halves?
Wait, when I had three objects (id=12, id=13, id=14), only id=12 was paintable. But id=13 was purple and id=14 was white. When SPACE was first pressed, id=13 (white, 100px) split into id=13 (purple, 50px) + id=14 (white, 50px). So the first half became purple and the second stayed white.
Hmm, so SPACE doesn't just split - it also changes the color of the top half to purple. Let me think about this more carefully.
Initial: id=12 (purple), id=13 (white, 100px)
After SPACE: id=12 (purple), id=13 (purple, 50px), id=14 (white, 50px)
So SPACE:
- Splits id=13 into two 50px objects
- The top half (id=13) becomes purple
- The bottom half (id=14) keeps the original color (white)
Then I painted id=12 white.
After SPACE again: id=12 (white), id=13 (white, 100px) - merged back, taking bottom color
So the merge takes the bottom color. And the split makes the top half purple.
I need: id=12 (white), id=13 (purple, 100px)
If I SPACE again to split, I'll get: id=12 (white), id=13 (purple, 50px), id=14 (white, 50px)
Then if I SPACE again, it merges: id=12 (white), id=13 (white, 100px) - takes bottom color
That doesn't help. I need a way to make the bottom half purple too.
Wait, maybe I need to look at this differently. What if there's a way to change id=13's color when it's split? Let me try pressing SPACE to split, then see if I can paint id=13 (not just id=12).
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the frame (id=11) can be moved. What if I use arrow keys AFTER splitting to move the frame to id=13, then paint? Let me try: SPACE to split, then DOWN to move frame, then paint.
But earlier, arrow keys only ticked the timer. However, maybe they work differently when there are 3 objects vs 2 objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE to split, then try arrow keys to move frame
action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [11, 12, 13, 14]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} [{b[0]}]-[{b[2] if len(b)>2 else b[0]}]")

# Now try DOWN to see if frame moves
frame_before = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['id'] == 11][0]['boundary']
action(['DOWN'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
frame_after = [n for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['id'] == 11][0]['boundary']
print(f"\nFrame moved: {frame_before != frame_after}")
print(f"  Before: {frame_before}")
print(f"  After: {frame_after}")

# Check all node changes
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    bn = [x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == n['id']]
    if bn:
        bn = bn[0]
        if bn['boundary'] != n['boundary'] or bn['color'] != n['color']:
            print(f"  id={n['id']}: color {bn['color']}->{n['color']}, boundary changed: {bn['boundary'] != n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Toggles: splits id=13 into purple(top)+original(bottom) or merges taking bottom color", "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Tick down timer only (so far)", "MOUSE on top-right indicator": "Paints id=12 with that color"}, "object_taxonomy": {"id_11": "Gray frame around id=12", "id_12": "Top center object (paintable)", "id_13": "Bottom center object(s)"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_strip": "Timer bar", "top_right": "Color palette"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "SPACE splits: id=13(white,100px) -> id=13(purple,50px)+id=14(white,50px). Merge takes bottom color. Need: id=12(white), id=13(purple,100px).", "goal_model": "Match center to left panel: white above purple", "recent_findings": ["SPACE is reversible toggle", "Split makes top half purple, merge takes bottom color"], "open_questions": ["Can arrow keys move frame when split?", "How to make bottom half purple?"], "current_plan": "SPACE to split, then try arrow keys to move frame to id=13", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
